speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape, uh, four, interviewing Malka Baran. Is there anything that, uh, comes to your mind that, uh, you would like to mention that we didn't cover in the previous tape?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, uh, no. I think we went fairly smoothly. I just talked about the time I cried for the first time when I thought about my parents. And later on, when we traveled through with the Russians, uh, that incident that I described, I called 'the little flame,' because my faith in human kindness was reignited again through their behavior. And we were with them. The life was quite, uh, content. I learned in photography to work, develop pictures. I made friends with Ukrainian girls and men. We worked together. Um--

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of pictures did you develop? Do you know what--?

speaker: interviewee
text: Their photography. Any pictures that they took of the soldiers, of, uh, of, uh-- group pictures. I was in the late-- laboratory. I was developing them in the-- in the, uh, liquid. And I learned the skill of that. And my friend worked in a shop for-- where they bought soldiers' clothes. And she was very, very active person. She actually directed my life all the time, till I became a human being again. Because until then, even with the Russian army, I still was passive, doing what the faith and people and Sheshanka told me to do. Uh, I still was not trying to open up inside or regain my freedom as a human being and learn to live again. We actually had to learn to live again. I did. And it came slowly.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you learn to be a human being again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, to follow the chronological events, when the war ended and we were standing outside, and how did we know about it? The soldiers started to scream, 'War is ended, war is ended!' Everybody ran outside. They celebrated, of course, drank, uh, sang. And I was standing under the tree and watching them. And I remember, and what now?

speaker: interviewer
text: What date-- what-- what was the date of it, do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was May 8, '45. That was the only day I remember clearly. It was the end of the war. And from then on, uh, things changed. Because they began to send Russian officers and soldiers home slowly, gradually.

speaker: interviewer
text: What went through your mind when the war was over? What was it like for you? Can you recall this?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, it was, of course, some kind of a joy, but not fully. Because I-- I knew I am alone and parents are not with me, and my family was all destroyed. But I was an observer at that time, more than participant yet. I observed around me, what's happening, I remember. I was observing people. I always loved to watch people. And I guess I was very interested in their behavior. What happened right after that is that my benefactor, that Isidore, was sent home without me. They did not allow him to take me. The major of the Russian army explained that he has to clarify if we are not spies. After all, we are from Poland. We cannot be sent to Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were there already in Germany, or you were in--

speaker: interviewee
text: --in Austria. That was Modling, M-O-D-L-I-N-G, Austria. Um, Sheshanka decided-- since we were left without him anyway, we continued working-- that, uh, since they started to send the Ukrainian girls who were also working in that outfit to Russia. And Sheshanka found out through some friendships with officers-- Jewish officers or Russian officers in that unit-- that those girls are not really sent home to their families, but sent home to Siberia.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sheshanka, for some reason-- maybe they didn't trust him-- Shesanka decided it's time for us to act and try to go leave the Russian army, go back to Poland. But she didn't want to go back to Poland. We found out-- she found out. I didn't know anything-- that there was a ro-- Rothschild Hospital in Germ-- in Germany, where they collect the Jewish, uh, survivors, refugees, to prepare them to send them to Israel. Now how do we leave the army and go there? That was a major thing. But she organized it. She spoke to a Jewish major. And she said, look, we are not Russians. We are Polish citizens. We request a permit to go back to Poland, where we belong. Ger-- um, Linz, I remember, was divided into four, uh, parts. Russian, American, English, and-- American, Russian, English, and French, maybe? I don't know. Four quads. Was this a city?

speaker: interviewee
text: A city. Big city.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Austria?

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- yeah. And each person that belonged to a certain country went there to that part to arrange with the consulate to return them. The Jewish major gave us a note, and we left the following morning. Sheshanka felt that it's dangerous for us to remain with them. And we went to that Rothschild place where they, uh, send people off to, uh camps, to displaced person camps to prepare them for the trip to Israel. And we were not even stationed-- usually Jews waited in that hospital, in the Rothschild Hospital. It was a gathering place to be sent to various camps, post-war camps. But we did not stay. Sheshanka said it's very dangerous. The Russians may change their minds and come for us.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they sent us the same evening-- we didn't sleep over yet-- in a train to Wegscheid, which was a Jewish-- uh, in Austria-- uh, a displaced person camp. We were on the train. We were driving some hours, and we arrived there. And before we left the train car, a young Jewish man jumps to the train and says, is anyone here who doesn't have a place to sleep? We said, two. So we went with him. And he took us to the barrack for the Betar organization. He was from Betar, and he was trying to collect people for his organization. Can you tell me something about what kind of organization is Betar?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband will tell you about it, because I-- I'm not an organization person. But it was, uh, an active, uh, Jewish organization for the safety and the fight for Israel. Um, we went there, and we got a place to sleep, a little room in that barrack. That's where I met Moshe later. And we stayed in that displaced person camp, and I worked with children-- children who were saved from war. They were either in the woods or bunkers or hidden. Young children. Made up-- we made up a school, and I was teaching them. Children from Romania, children from different countries I didn't know the language. I knew a little bit of Hebrew from my school, previous, uh, education.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we-- I worked with them by-- with sign language, training them to certain words in Hebrew, and with love. And they returned love. And that was when I began to return to my 'normal,' quote, unquote, life and decision making and laughing and singing again, with children. So they brought me back.

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, we stayed in that camp until I left for Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh, let me go back a little bit to the time when you were with the Russian army, with your benefactor. During the time when you were traveling, did you have any communications with him? Did you talk to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were working at the same place. Not when we traveled to him, no, except for that note. But when we traveled with the army, he was still with us. But shortly after, he was working. We met in the evening. He was so good and kind, and he was so happy. And he told his wife that he's bringing a Jewish girl that he saved. And-- and he was really excited about it. Then they sent him away, and we stayed with the Russian army. And I never heard from him again. We couldn't communicate. I didn't even know his address.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were talking about the children you were teaching. And what was-- what was the next, uh--

speaker: interviewee
text: We were-- I was working with the children in first grade, helping them, also, to grow through the experience, through the trauma. Learning to sing together, to dance together, to learn the language a little bit.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were the children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Um, six, seven, five. You know, the classes were not organized, so young children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were these children whose parents, uh, were killed or these were children--

speaker: interviewee
text: Some of them had parents. They survived in bunkers and hiding places. Some survived on Aryan papers, as non-Jews. Uh, some of them were not alone. Most of them were with some people. And we organized the camp for them in the summer, and I worked with them. And they began to return to me, uh, sort of more stable, regular, thinking about maybe there is life after. You know, that everything will be all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe-- can you describe those children after the war? What-- what were they like before you began to teach them?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were like most children, but they were traumatized. But they-- they were enjoying singing. And they responded to love. And I could give them a lot. And we're sort of helping each other grow. Some of them were frightened, but after a while, they saw that they are in a safe situation. There are only Jewish people, kind people. There are no Germans around. There are no hiding places. They enjoyed the outdoors. So did we. Um, we had a [POLISH], a summer place for them that was organized later, when summer came. They-- they slept. And I went from child to child, kissing them good night, washing them, giving them a bath. It was like giving and taking. Giving and taking. We helped each other. And it was a wonderful period.

speaker: interviewee
text: And from then on, on my birthday, every time my birthday arrived, I was in a different place, a different situation. I always thought, where would I be, what will I be next January? And slowly, I returned to life. I came to Israel. Of course, I met Leah, um, my sort of adopted mother in Austria-- a teacher who was sent. There was a group of teachers in the army. [INTERPOSING VOICES] What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Leah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was her last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fischman. Well, [NON-ENGLISH] her maiden name at that time. She was serving the Israeli army. And as a teacher, she was sent to displaced person camps to help organize schools and help teachers. And we were standing, uh, opposite each other-- we, those teachers that were not yet teachers but worked with children. And the teachers opposite us. And I sort of liked her, the way she looked. And something drew me to her. And she said later that she hoped that they'll assign her to me. And I hoped that they'll assign her to me, too. And we were assigned to each other, and since then, it was my family, actually. I came to Israel. I came to her house. She arranged school for me. She arranged later work for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in Austr-- in Austria?

speaker: interviewee
text: Until '48.

speaker: interviewer
text: In 1948? And how did you-- what was the next, uh, event?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came to Israel in '48, where the bombs were falling still. And we had to hide in the bunkers because of the War of Independence.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you travel--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: --to Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, illegally. Through trains and walk. And-- and trains, actually, and walking. And I don't remember. It was like in a dream.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which-- trains from Austria took you to where?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Austria to Greece, could be? I-- I don't remember. I went through many countries. Some on foot through the mountains, some on trains, and finally we came to Israel on a boat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the boat?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was a freight boat, by the way. It was not a ship. Because we slept on benches on the deck. And there were no cabins, to speak of. And I was in illegal papers, and I forgot-- in the train, somebody came to check my name, and I forgot that I am a different name. And I said my previous name, and he said, what? It's something else. We got the papers through the Bricha.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is a Bricha?

speaker: interviewee
text: Organization that helped Jews from-- survivors to go to Israel. Israel was not a legal country yet, and they didn't allow us--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name for Israel before?

speaker: interviewee
text: Palestine. But in '48, when the War of Independence occurred, still they were not allowed. The Engl-- English government did not allow people to come. So on illegal papers we came.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your trip, uh, on the ship? How long was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know how long it was, but I remember we were sleeping on the deck and, uh, conditions were very poor. And, um--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there a lot of people on the ship?

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot of people, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the city that you came to in what was then Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came-- I came early in the morning. Um--

speaker: interviewer
text: No, it was already Israel when you came.

speaker: interviewee
text: Israel, right. I came earl-- early beginning. There was still some fighting. Jerusalem was-- [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: What part of--

speaker: interviewee
text: --still [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: --the year was it when you came to Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Summer. Or spring, because I remember wearing, uh, clothes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your feeling? Uh, what-- what was it like for you when you landed in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Uh, I kissed the ground, like everybody else. We came to a beach--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: --early in the morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: What city was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember that. Somewhere near Ra'anana, maybe. Uh, we came early in the morning, and I was not familiar with the cities of Israel or places. And there were not barracks, but [NON-ENGLISH], tents on the beach. And each one of us was taken to a tent. And before I had time to realize where I was and what it was, somebody called my name on the speaker. I forgot to mention that a-- a distant relative of mine that lived in Israel, searching for relatives in Poland, found through some friends of mine-- I don't exactly know how-- that I survived. And he sent me those false papers to take me as a-- a proxy wife to Israel. And he was in the army at that time. And he sent his friend with a little girl, five years old, [NON-ENGLISH], to greet me at that beach. And that's why my ca-- my name was called. And I go out, and I meet that little, tiny, sweet little girl, five years old, with her daddy. And I don't know them. And they said, we are friends of that and that and [NON-ENGLISH] Klin, who was my cousin, distant cousin. And we are taking you to our house because he's in the army. So I went with them to their house, and I stayed with them two or three weeks and through the holidays. Oh, it must have been, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] um, early fall. Because I spent September with them-- Rosh Hashanah. And since then, we became very, very close friends. And then I went to meet my cousin. That, uh-- the proxy was annulled, of course. It was not legal thing. So I worked-- I stayed with them for a while. Then I was accepted to a seminary for teachers in Jerusalem. I stayed in an apartment with an older, aristocratic, Russian, uh, dame-- lady, like 80 years old. She gave me, uh, bread and board. And I worked for her. I served her breakfast, I washed the dishes, I did housework, and I studied. I was accepted to seminary without a penny. Uh, became very, very close and friendly with the classmates, my friends, who are still very close with me. And that's how I began to study and slowly go back to life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where-- where did you meet your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Austria, in that displaced person camp, where I was in a Betari group, and he was a Betari because he belonged to them. He used to come to the barrack to meet with his supervisors-- or co-supervisors.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your husband's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Moshe.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was his last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Baran. And that-- that was in our, uh, in our barrack. And the friend of his introduced me to Moshe. And we came very close. First, we called ourselves [NON-ENGLISH], sister and brother, and then it developed into something serious.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you know each other before you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he was, uh, staying in Austria while I was sent to Israel. He was supposed to follow to Israel with his family. His mother and sister and brother survived the war because he stayed in the-- in the woods as a partisan. And, uh, his, uh, mother did not want to go to Israel, because she was afraid. She lost one daughter and a husband in the war. All the children were, uh, army age. She was afraid they'll go to the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they went to America. And we were separated for six years, and wrote to each other. And then when he settled with his parents-- with his family there-- he came to Israel. And we married in Israel. Where did you marry?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Leah's house, in my adopted family's house, the one that, uh, they adopted me in Austria-- the teacher.

speaker: interviewer
text: What city did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ramat Gan. She still lives in the apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your husband propose to you? How did this happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, maybe it's time for us to stop being brother and sister. And I said, fine. And that was the proposal.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we married, I was 25.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Moshe?

speaker: interviewee
text: He's seven years older, so he was 32.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you got married, where did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, I continued in Israel. I was working as a teacher already. I finished the seminary. And he went back to America. It took 10 months to get papers for me. So I was still working in Israel, in Herzliya. And when he sent the papers, I left Israel and I went to New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have children?

speaker: interviewee
text: We have two daughters, married with grandchildren, thank god. Six wonderful grandchildren, and two of our daughters-- one is an educator, one is an artist.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your daughters were born where?

speaker: interviewee
text: Both were born in Brooklyn, in Maimonides Hospital. And, uh, went to school in Queens.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, uh, were-- are your daughters here in-- in Pittsburgh?

speaker: interviewee
text: One is in Pittsburgh. One lives in Israel. So we travel between the two. By the way, I, uh, got my education continuously in Queens. I got my bachelor's degree and master's degree in special education. Bachelor's in early childhood.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh, do you--

speaker: interviewee
text: So I graduated with my daughter together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your trip to the States? Did you know English at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: A little bit. Uh, I studied English at home a little bit and after the camps a little bit. I even taught a little bit of English in the B camp, in the displaced person camp. And when I came here, I studied, uh, very seriously in college, though I-- and I like to read, so I read English books. That helped me a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long was the trip from Israel to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that was easy. That was two weeks on a very elegant ship. It was not like the first time.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did it feel for you to come to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: Happy and painful. I didn't want to leave Israel. I wanted to stay there. Israel gave me a lot of love and acceptance and friends and new families. And I felt that I not only owed them that after what happened to us, I wanted to live there. But I was alone. My whole family was destroyed and murdered. The extended family, my immediate family. And Moshe had his mother and sister and brother. And they were very close. And I did not feel that I have the right to separate them. His mother would not go. I didn't-- he-- if I would ask him, he maybe would agree, because he was very much for living in Israel, too. But I didn't have the heart even to say. Knowing how the family was close and loving, I decided that I'll go. So I was happy coming to my husband, whom I loved very much and still do, and I was unhappy leaving Israel. As a result, as you'll see later, our older daughter took over, was always interested in Israel, was interested in Holocaust, and made aliyah to Israel after going there every summer. Decided to stay there, remain for life there.

speaker: interviewer
text: She lives in Israel. Does she have children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are the girls' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: One is Maya, and one is Avia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what are the names of your-- what is the name of your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Children here?

speaker: interviewer
text: --daughter who lives here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Avi and Paul. And they have four children. Beginning with 12-year-old, every three years down. Yossi is the first one. Boaz is the second one. Uh, Isaac is the third one. And Elyiana-- Eli Anna, which is 'God answered'-- is the fourth one.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you come to Pittsburgh?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three years ago. I'm beginning my fourth year. In October was three years.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what was the reason that you came to Pittsburgh?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we worked in New York all our lives. And I was teaching, Moshe was working. And when we retired, it was a question-- one year we remained as retirees in New York. Then we had to travel to Israel to be with our daughter there. We had to travel to Pittsburgh to stay with our children here. We decided one place or the other must be our home, because it doesn't make sense for us to travel. And we chose Pittsburgh.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that you have worked as a teacher in where? In--?

speaker: interviewee
text: I work in a private Jewish nursery school. I call it-- not really teaching. I don't respond to that very well. I call it helping children grow. I was working as a teacher-director, first as a teacher, then as a teacher-director in a small nursery school. And had a wonderful program which I developed myself, which I'm writing about now, to describe my work.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many years have you worked there?

speaker: interviewee
text: As a teacher I worked 35 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: But in this particular--

speaker: interviewee
text: --and director. In particular school, for 25. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, we are coming to an end of this, uh, testimony of yours. Uh, can you tell me, why do you think it was important for you to give this testimony?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- I think it's extremely important for many reasons. First of all-- [RINGING] --to leave-- that's not ours. To leave testimony of what we went through, although there are people that, uh, really, uh, claim that it never happened. I wanted, also, for future generations to see, that even though we went through hell-- through very difficult, terribly disturbing times and were at the pit, there is always hope. There is always the possibility for change, especially for young people who are desperate for some reason, justifiably. So I wanted to show them, here I am. I became an educator. I became a parent. I raised a family. I still have faith in people. I still have hope for the future. I still love people. It's important to know, and-- that there is always a change in life. Don't ever despair because it's the end of the rope. There's always a change, and there's also a rebirth that you don't destroy everything. There's always a spark of life that with kindness of people, and with open mind, you can change. I also wanted to show the world, and especially young people-- I go to schools and I talked about it-- that hate and prejudice are extremely dangerous. And they completely, um, disturb a person to see life as it could be and as it is. And it is very important to uproot it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- why do you think-- what helps you to survive, uh, your war experience?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a very difficult question to answer. Because I would say my personality, but I don't know. I often think if I wouldn't go through what I went through, would I be the same person? I always had interest in people. I still have it. I was always optimistic, and I still am. I always loved reading, and I still read, and I learned from it. I always loved learning, and I'm still learning. But I think my experience made me-- perhaps, I'm not sure, because maybe I would be the same if I didn't go through experience-- maybe a deeper person and more compassionate, understanding, perhaps. And understanding that desperation and bad conditions are not the end, and it can change. Also understanding that human spirit and the life within you and the mind, if it works, can really perform miracles and regain its strength and its life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think faith had, uh, some part in your survival?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's interesting. Because as I said before, in my childhood I had this relationship with God, but I didn't know much about Judaism. In the camp, I don't know if I thought about it. But after the camp, I returned to the same procedure, and I began to study Judaism, which I didn't know much. And as I-- and I continuously study now. I don't belong to any particular-- I go to synagogue, of course-- but I don't belong to-- I don't call myself orthodox, conservative, or reformed. I call myself a learning Jewess. And there is so much to learn and so much that I didn't know about humanity, about relationships, about how to live. And there's so much beauty in it.

speaker: interviewee
text: What kept me alive? Maybe God. Maybe coincidence. Maybe a miracle. Because I didn't do anything to survive. So I cannot really explain it. I was so passive and so closed. I feel that everything was flowing over me on the outside, but inside was locked. And still, some period of my time is locked. So it's hard to say what kept me alive, maybe. Is there anything-- [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: I did not. I did not do much.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would be your message to the world?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said it before-- uh, uproot the prejudice and the hatred. Learn to know people for what they are. Do not des-- despair when something terrible happens, because there is always change.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you for giving your testimony.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: Perfect. So now--
